_meta.methodology_note: Environmental-impact grades are a capacity-weighted average across each provider's known AI data center sites. See 'How we grade this' for full methodology.

sites.name: Prometheus
sites.water_note: No Meta-specific figure is public. The only known number is an aggregate across all New Albany business-park tenants (Meta, Google, others): ~8M gal/day peak draw as of Apr 2026, against 16M gal/day contracted capacity. Not attributable to Meta alone.

sources.name: Measured AI — Meta Prometheus & Google Ohio primer
Epoch AI — Meta Prometheus facility page

sites.name: Colossus 1
sites.water_note: Real, trending daily figures exist, but only because a local watchdog group (Protect Our Aquifer) obtained MLGW utility billing data — not because xAI disclosed anything. Usage roughly doubled in nine months: 381,000 gal/day (Aug 2025) to 812,502 gal/day (May 2026). A promised water-recycling plant that would cut ~3M gal/day of drinking-water draw has paused construction as of April 2026.

sources.name: Memphis Flyer — Protect Our Aquifer MLGW analysis
Daily Memphian — xAI/MLGW bill analysis
The Cooldown — water-recycling plan stalled

sites.name: Colossus 2
sites.water_note: MLGW (the public utility, not xAI) has stated the facility is expected to use up to 1,000,000 gal/day once fully operational. The company has not itself disclosed a figure.

sources.name: Inside Climate News — Colossus 2 permitting coverage

sites.name: New Carlisle Campus
sites.water_note: More voluntary disclosure than most, but no confirmed absolute operational figure. AWS has publicly described its cooling methodology (outside-air cooling most of the year; water used only a few hours/day April–September, none Oct–March) and cited an industry LBNL water-efficiency benchmark. A company rep separately told local news the site draws from a capped regional pool (24M gal/day cap for all regional industrial users) and expects to use water only ~2% of operating hours. Separately — and not the same figure — the county approved a construction-phase dewatering discharge permit for up to 31M gal/day; that's a one-time construction impact, not ongoing operations. Local reporting has documented well and pond depletion attributed to the dewatering phase.

sources.name: AWS — Project Rainier announcement
WSBT — Amazon rep Q&A on water
WSBT — county dewatering discharge permit
Sierra Club Indiana — Hoosier Data Center blog series

sites.name: Fairwater (Mount Pleasant)
sites.water_note: Disclosure came only after a fight: Microsoft initially sought to have its Lake Michigan water-use records classified as trade secrets; environmental groups (Milwaukee Riverkeeper, Midwest Environmental Advocates) sued the City of Racine after a 210-day records-request delay. Microsoft then withdrew the trade-secret claim and a figure became public: up to 8.4M gallons/year, with 6M gallons returned after treatment. Separately, Microsoft's CEO has since claimed a newer closed-loop cooling design (used at this site) uses water 'equivalent to a single restaurant per year' — a company claim not yet independently audited. The New York Times also reported a gap between Microsoft's internal 2030 global water-use forecast (~28 billion liters) and the lower figure (~18 billion liters) the company gave the Times on the record.

sources.name: Wisconsin Public Radio — records lawsuit & 8.4M gal figure
Progressive.org — trade-secret framing
Tom's Hardware — Nadella 'restaurant' claim, unaudited

sites.name: Council Bluffs
sites.water_note: Google is the only major operator to self-report water per data center — its 2024 Environmental Report breaks out ~24 US/Canada sites. Council Bluffs is its thirstiest: ~1.3B gallons of potable water in 2024 (roughly 2.8M gal/day consumed, 3.9M gal/day withdrawn), on evaporative cooling. Figures vary by source and metric (~1.0B consumed / 1.3B potable / 1.4B withdrawn). The disclosure comes from the company itself. (Site power ~350 MW is a third-party estimate — Google does not publish per-site MW.)

sources.name: Google — Iowa data center location & stewardship
MOST Policy Initiative — Data Center Water Use
DC Hub — Google Council Bluffs facility (power estimate)

sites.name: Stargate — Abilene
sites.water_note: One physical facility, listed once to avoid double-counting: Oracle operates it, Crusoe built it, and OpenAI is the AI tenant. Cooling is one-time-fill closed-loop (non-evaporative). Oracle and the City of Abilene put the initial fill at ~8M gallons of city water (against the city's ~22M gal/day total draw), with only periodic top-ups and maintenance drains after. Neither company has disclosed a precise ongoing operational figure — OpenAI's public framing stays qualitative ('initial fill about two Olympic pools; annual use like a medium office building'). Borderline partial/opaque: a real (initial-fill) number is public, but the figure that matters — steady-state consumption — is not. Powered by on-site natural gas plus grid (including some wind).

sources.name: Oracle — Abilene data center
Epoch AI — OpenAI Stargate: where the US sites stand
OpenAI — Building the compute infrastructure (closed-loop claim)
Texas Tribune — Data centers thirsty for Texas' water

sites.name: Polaris Forge 1 (Ellendale)
sites.water_note: CoreWeave is an AI-specialist cloud that mostly leases capacity (~850 MW active across ~41 sites at end-2025) rather than owning it, so site-level attribution is murky. Its flagship campus — Polaris Forge 1 in Ellendale, ND — is built by Applied Digital and leased by CoreWeave (~400 MW across phases). CoreWeave publishes no water or WUE figures for it or any site, and no utility/watchdog figure has surfaced — nothing public. Capacity here is leased and not cleanly attributable, so the site is graded but excluded from the capacity-weighted average.

sources.name: CoreWeave — AI Data Centers
Applied Digital — additional 150 MW North Dakota lease to CoreWeave
CoreWeave 10-K (FY2025), SEC EDGAR

sites.name: Hyperion
sites.water_note: Meta's largest campus yet — a $50B+ build ramping to ~2 GW by 2030 and ~5 GW at full buildout (~2036), so little is operational today. Meta hasn't put this in an environmental report, but real numbers are public via state water-registration records and Meta's own projections (reported by nola.com and WBRZ): up to ~23M gal/day is registered as the maximum, while Meta projects ~1.5M gal/day — 500–600M gal/year — in normal operation once running in 2028, cycled through a closed-loop system and drawn from the Mississippi River Alluvial Aquifer. Critics note sustained pumping near the 23M/day maximum could drop local groundwater 65+ feet. Graded partial: the figures reach the public through state records and local reporting, not a Meta disclosure. Shown for scale; excluded from the weighted average until it has meaningful operating capacity.

sources.name: Meta — Richland Parish data center (company page)
NOLA.com — How much water will Meta's Louisiana data center use?
WBRZ — Meta's Louisiana data center water-usage concern
Sierra Club — In rural Louisiana, Meta's data center at what cost

sites.name: Fairwater (Atlanta)
sites.water_note: Microsoft's second Fairwater site — built by QTS with Microsoft as the tenant (a colocation arrangement), wired to the Wisconsin campus as one 'AI superfactory'; ~636 MW of IT capacity operational per Epoch AI, ramping past 700 by end-2026. Microsoft describes a closed-loop liquid-cooling design and cites fleet-wide WUE, but no verifiable water figure is published for this specific site — Epoch's facility record lists none, and neither Microsoft nor QTS has released one; all that exists is Microsoft's qualitative 'closed-loop uses about as much water as a restaurant per year' line. That's less than even the Abilene site, which at least has an ~8M-gal initial-fill number, so on the page's own rule (a closed-loop site with no published number scores opaque) Atlanta is opaque. Because Atlanta (636 MW) is larger than Mount Pleasant (400 MW), capacity-weighting pulls Microsoft's overall grade to opaque — and with Atlanta verified above 400 MW that flip is robust, not a knife-edge.

sources.name: Epoch AI — Microsoft Fairwater Atlanta (facility record; QTS-built, 636 MW)
Microsoft — From Wisconsin to Atlanta: first AI superfactory
Data Center Frontier — Microsoft's Fairwater Atlanta
Measured AI — Fairwater Atlanta (grid-only gigawatt)

sites.name: Canton Campus (Madison County)
sites.water_note: The Mississippi half of Project Rainier — the other Anthropic-dedicated Trainium2 build: >350 MW of IT capacity today, ramping past 1 GW, powered by Entergy Mississippi plus new solar farms. Here Amazon has actually put numbers out (via local reporting): the Canton campus is projected to use ~83M gallons/year, drawn from Canton Municipal Utilities and set to shift entirely to recycled wastewater (Madison County Wastewater Authority, with Veolia) by 2027, paired with an aquifer-replenishment program targeting ~150M gal/year of offsets. More disclosure than most — but still a forward projection surfaced largely through local outlets rather than an audited company environmental report, so partial rather than transparent.

sources.name: Amazon — $25B Mississippi investment (addresses water usage)
Mississippi Today — Where Amazon's Mississippi data centers stand
News From The States — Amazon details project status, water & energy
Semafor — AWS' multi-state AI data center powering Claude

sites.name: The Dalles
sites.water_note: The textbook 'pried loose' case: The Dalles's water figures became public only after The Oregonian sued the city and, following a multi-year fight Google helped resist, a settlement forced annual disclosure. The numbers are real and trending — 461.1M gal withdrawn / 361.4M gal consumed in 2024, ~550M gal in 2025 (~40% of the city's total supply). Google now also reports per-site water in its own environmental report (which is why its flagship Council Bluffs scores transparent); The Dalles is graded partial to reflect that here the figure was surfaced by litigation, not volunteered. No per-site MW is published, so it is shown but not capacity-weighted.

sources.name: Latitude Media — data-center water 'black box,' Google
Reynolds Center — The Oregonian exposes data-center water use
The Register — Google, The Dalles water use
